issue_title: Multiple Providers Call to highest block number
issue_body:
* Version: latest
* Python:3.5
* OS: win

### What was wrong?
I think it is wrong (not 100% )! Right now I'm using infura as HTTP Provider and want to add a localhost provider running geth. So the natural way would be: 
`web3 = Web3([geth_local_host_provider, infura_provider])`

Now as I understand web3 makes now always the first request to geth_local_host_provider and if it fails it tries infura_provider. In my case sometimes geth get out of sync. So the actual block number is different. I think the right strategy would be to make the "request" to the HTTP Provider with the highest block.

### How can it be fixed?
Check first the block's of every provider and filter if any provider in the list have a lower block number. 

Fill this section in if you know how this could or should be fixed.

issue_title: No event logs are returned since 4.6.0
issue_body:
* Version: 4.6.0
* Python: 3.6.5
* OS: win


### What was wrong?

In version 4.6.0, filtering on contract events returns no logs when the contract address is set (using ganache-cli). It still fails after updating eth-abi to the alpha 2.0 version. Version 4.5.0 works.

The following code fails to return logs in 4.6.0:

```python
contract = web3.eth.contract(
    abi=abi,
    address=address
)
event_filter = contract.events.SomeEvent.createFilter(
    fromBlock=0,
    toBlock='latest'
)
event_filter.get_all_entries()
> []
```

The same code works fine in 4.5.0, returning an array of event dicts as expected.

Event logs are also returned in 4.6.0 when initializing the contract without an address:

```python
contract = web3.eth.contract(
    abi=abi
)
```

### How can it be fixed?

I have identified the issue is caused by the changes made in f1e8e915. Specifically these two lines:

https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/commit/f1e8e91503153c9942db570ac88752fd63d0a278#diff-f1ec2154438a2971d779c561901b6591R27
https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/commit/f1e8e91503153c9942db570ac88752fd63d0a278#diff-480e77d839a1bfb9e5557e00f9b9c7aeR16

Changing

https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/blob/master/web3/utils/rpc_abi.py#L27

back to `'address': 'address'`, and 

https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/blob/master/web3/middleware/normalize_request_parameters.py#L16

to `'address': apply_formatter_if(is_string, lambda x: x)})` solves the issue, though this is probably not the desired fix.

issue_title: Uninstall subtlety & how I fixed a Watchman hang
issue_body:
First off wanted to say thanks to everyone involved in this excellent app!

This issue involves both the Homebrew [formula](https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-core/blob/44b5c7447c6aab5af310b6eac0f76d56e180cc7c/Formula/watchman.rb) and `watchman`.

Today something in my `watchman` state dir caused a hang[1] just like https://github.com/facebook/watchman/issues/358#issue-176593548.
I also solved by https://github.com/facebook/watchman/issues/358#issuecomment-246672605.

Confusingly (to me), `brew uninstall watchman` didn't touch that directory.
On the one hand, it would have saved me a few hours today. On the other hand, @wez based on your https://github.com/facebook/watchman/issues/358#issuecomment-246779161 it sounds like that folder is potentially valuable for debugging.

Inside I see a log file (helpful) but also pid/state/socket, the inclusion of which in `brew uninstall`  could have saved me this trouble.

@jerzerak, @wez, (any other maintainers): thoughts? how do other packages handle this? 
Is there a `brew uninstall -complete $PACKAGE` or something?
My naive thought: feels like a good compromise may be retaining/leaving the logfile but removing the rest.

We sometimes get an exception `termios.error: (4, 'Interrupted system call')` propagating out of pyserial's `serial.serialposix.flush`.

`tcdrain` can fail with EINTR if some signal was handled, see http://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man3/tcdrain.3p.html .

We are handling this application-side by catching `termios.error` and retrying. But since https://github.com/pyserial/pyserial/blob/master/serial/serialposix.py#L514 it looks like the idea is that pyserial should be handling EINTR, perhaps a retry for flush/tcdrain should be added?

Note: none of the other tc* functions appear to return EINTR.

Note2: Python 3.5 apparently retries a lot of syscalls https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0475/ automatically, but from glancing at cpython's 3.5 branch it doesn't look like termios.tcdrain was given this treatment.

issue_title: sshuttle doesn't hide my IPV6 address
issue_body:
I've been using sshuttle for a number of years now, and recently my ISP rolled out IPV6 support. 
The sshuttle command I use is:

    sshuttle --no-latency-control -vvr username@remote.server 0/0 -x 10.0.0.0/24

Sometimes I add the ````--dns```` flag as well.

This has been working fine, and continuous to work fine, for sites that show me an IPV4 address. For example, http://www.whatsmyip.org/ correctly shows me remote.server's address, instead of my real one. And I see what looks like correct activity from the sshuttle debug output.

However I've recently noticed that sites responding back with an IPV6 (have IPV6 support?) are still showing me my real IPV6 address. For example:

    https://www.google.com/search?q=google+what%27s+my+ip&ie=utf-8&oe=utf-8
    icanhazip.com

When accessing those sites or downloading anything from them I see no output from sshuttle, which makes me suspect sshuttle is getting bypassed somehow and doesn't intercept the IPV6 traffic (although I am just guessting). 
Furthermore if the ````--dns```` flag is used I do see the dns query go through for those sites, just not the traffic. My best guess for this would be that whichever DNS I'm using isn't configured for IPV6, and so sshuttle still intercepts the traffic.

issue_title: uint pack and unpack don't work for non-power of 2 byte_sizes
issue_body:
On a little-endian Windows machine, be_uint_fields with a size of 3 are not properly packed and unpacked. I believe this would be the case on other platforms too, and for any byte_size not handled by the standard struct pack stuff.

The followng snippet demonstrates the problem:

```
from infi.instruct.buffer import (Buffer, be_uint_field, le_uint_field, buffer_field,
                                  list_field, str_field)
from infi.instruct.buffer.macros import bytes_ref

class TestBufferLE(Buffer):
    three_byte_int = le_uint_field(where=bytes_ref[0:3])
    
class TestBufferBE(Buffer):
    three_byte_int = be_uint_field(where=bytes_ref[0:3])
    
tle = TestBufferLE()
tle.three_byte_int = 0x012345

print 'little-endian:'
print ':'.join(['%02x' % x for x in tle.pack()])

tle2 = TestBufferLE()
tle2.unpack(tle.pack())
print hex(tle2.three_byte_int)

print '*****************************************'

tbe = TestBufferBE()
tbe.three_byte_int = 0x012345

print 'big-endian:'
print ':'.join(['%02x' % x for x in tbe.pack()])
tbe2 = TestBufferBE()
tbe2.unpack(tbe.pack())
print hex(tbe2.three_byte_int)
```

The code above generates the following output:
```
little-endian
45:23:01
0x12345
*****************************************
big-endian
45:23:01
0x12345

```
It should generate:

```
little-endian:
45:23:01
0x12345
*****************************************
big-endian:
01:23:45
0x12345

```

The following patch corrects the problem:
```
--- serialize.py        2016-12-13 15:30:07 -0500
+++ serialize_fixed.py  2016-12-13 15:37:33 -0500
@@ -79,17 +79,24 @@


 def pack_int(value, **kwargs):
+    from sys import byteorder
     byte_size = kwargs_fractional_byte_size(kwargs)
     if byte_size in STRUCT_INT_PACKERS:
         return STRUCT_INT_PACKERS[byte_size](value, **kwargs)
     else:
         byte_size = kwargs.pop("byte_size", float(value.bit_length()) / 8)
-        return pack_bit_int(value, byte_size, **kwargs)
+        result = pack_bit_int(value, byte_size, **kwargs)
+        if byteorder != kwargs.get("endian"):
+            result.reverse()
+        return result


 def unpack_bit_int(buffer, byte_size, **kwargs):
     result = 0
-    l = reversed(buffer[0:byte_size]) if kwargs.get("endian", "big") else buffer[0:byte_size]
+    if kwargs.get("endian") != 'big':
+        l = reversed(buffer[0:byte_size])
+    else:
+        l = buffer[0:byte_size]
     for b in l:
         result *= 256
         result += b
@@ -299,3 +306,4 @@
         offset += item_len
         index += 1
     return result, offset
+

```

issue_title: Flask module doesn't teardown app context
issue_body:
Hi,

The Flask module doesn't teardown the appcontext, which can lead to various issues when using Flask's appcontexts extensively.

- The context is not cleared and deactivated? which can lead to memory leaks.
- When using the `ThreadedRequestsHTTPTransport`, the `AsyncWorker` is not stopped which can lead to threads leakages.

I would say that we should add something like this in the `init_app` function:
```python
if hasattr(app, 'teardown_appcontext'):  # Support Flask < 0.9
    @app.teardown_appcontext
    def teardown(exc):
        # Avoid memory leak
        self.client.context.clear(deactivate=True)

        # Avoid threads leak
        transport = self.remote.get_transport()
        if isinstance(ThreadedRequestsHTTPTransport, transport):
            transport.get_worker().stop()
```

or is this just unsupported? Shouldn't it be documented somewhere? If find it quite surprising and unexpected.

Thanks!

issue_title: Event values for captureException are reversed
issue_body:
# Problem

When handling exceptions thrown from previous exceptions the order of the chain sent to sentry is incorrect.

See attached screenshots for the Sentry UI for an error thrown by this code:
```python
class FirstError(Exception):
    pass


class SecondError(Exception):
    pass


class ThirdError(Exception):
    pass

…

try:
    raise FirstError()
except Exception as e:
    try:
        raise SecondError() from e
    except Exception as e:
        raise ThirdError() from e
```

# Screenshots

![screen shot 2016-10-27 at 13 26 11](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/623271/19765555/53f7d868-9c49-11e6-849e-16997713643b.png)

![screen shot 2016-10-27 at 13 26 21](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/623271/19765562/59b3b0f6-9c49-11e6-9dea-3ea116fd6265.png)

# Expected outcome

* The event list in sentry should show `ThirdError` as title
* The order of the events should, while sorted with most recent first, be:
  * `ThirdError`
  * `SecondError`
  * `FirstError`

issue_body: Comparé a la calculette en ligne, on a des erreurs entre 100 et 300 euros, cf graph ci joint (x=salaire_imposable, y=erreur)

On est en train de réfléchir à inclure l'aide pour l'embauche d'un premier salarié dans le simulateur de coût d'embauche. 
@benjello  Est-ce que tu penses qu'on peut tout simplement l'inclure dans la variable [salsuperbrut](http://legislation.openfisca.fr/variables/salsuperbrut) ? 
La description de cette variable est `Salaires superbruts/coût du travail`. Cette prime est déterminante dans le coût du travail (500 euros tous les 3 mois), mais pas forcément à inclure dans un super brut car il faut en faire la demande, et qu'elle est délivrée sous conditions. 
Pourtant, si on décide de ne pas l'inclure, et donc par exemple de créer une variable `cout_du_travail` différente de `salsuperbrut`, je pense qu'il faudrait y mettre le CICE, qui lui aussi est accessible sous conditions et imputé de façon complexe.

Bonjour,

Un petit problème avec salaire_net_a_payer , salaire_net qui renvoie des valeurs négatives pour des salaire_de_base proche de 0.  ( au moins ]0, 100] ).

Test yaml ci-dessous : 

- name: "Différent de salaire_de_base"
  period: 2015-03
  input_variables:
    salaire_de_base: 100
  output_variables:
    sali: 0
    salaire_net_a_payer: 0 # TODO: connais pas la valeur exacte, mais supposée > 0
    salaire_net: 0 # TODO: connais pas la valeur exacte, mais supposée > 0
  period: 2015-03
  input_variables:
    salaire_de_base: 100
  output_variables:
    sali: 0
    salaire_net_a_payer: 0 # TODO: connais pas la valeur exacte, mais supposée > 0
    salaire_net: 0 # TODO: connais pas la valeur exacte, mais supposée > 0

J'essaie de comprendre pourquoi OpenFisca et le [Simulateur de Cout d'Embauche](https://embauche.beta.gouv.fr/simulateur/) ne donnent pas la même réponse pour le "coût du travail" en partant d'un même salaire de base de 2300€

Dans les deux cas c'est la "simulation minimale": la valeur renvoyée par OpenFisca est celle qu'on peut lire quand on fait [le test avec curl après une install](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/blob/master/README.md#servez-openfisca-france-avec-lapi-web-openfisca) réussie; la valeur du simulateur est celle obtenue avant toute interaction avec le formulaire.

Une partie de la différence s'explique par la prise en compte du CICE. Le simulateur d'embauche rapporte deux chiffres: avant prise en compte du CICE (3196€) et après (3035€).

Dans OpenFisca la variable `cout_du_travail` intègre le CICE et c'est donc 3078€.

La différence résiduelle tient à ce que le simulateur d'embauche intègre deux réductions: allègement Fillon et réductions d'allocations familiales; et deux cotisations, la formation professionnelle ainsi qu'une complémentaire santé arbitrairement fixée à 20€. (Dans ce cas précis le simulateur explique que c'est un exemple et propose d'affiner la description de sa situation, donc pour ces 20€ l'écart est compréhensible.)

Les trois autres variables ne sont pas présentes dans le calcul réalisé par OpenFisca, et ça comble l'écart. Mais maintenant je me demande pourquoi OpenFisca n'intègre pas ces trois variables dans le calcul, ou pourquoi (et comment) le simulateur d'embauche les intègre, dans la mesure où ça semble être la même requête vers OpenFisca.

issue_title: Add a data processor abstraction to process raw data and convert it into a list of Records in the runners.

This is to convert one line in the raw data to the format that blurr understands (as the Record class).

The simple case is when the input file has one json dict per line that corresponds to one event. For more complex cases one input line could correspond to multiple events (like in ipfix format). The data processor is an abstraction to handle that in the runner.

Questions:
 - default converter writes number in Fortran formatting (0.100D+06, 0.0000000E+00), while python is using different formatting. Fluka is also using Fortran formatting. Should it be kept  ?
Pros: easier comparison of text output with Fluka (making plain text diff)
Cons: for users it probably doesn't matter, fortran formatting is time consuming when converting large files, extra code can be removed from repo.

- default converter keeps header in same way as bdo2txt. Removing compatibility allows us to redesign header field.

- we have now two converters producing ASCII files: default and plotdata. They can be merge into one and decision on the number of columns moved to a switch. Lets take 1-D scoring (i.e. dose profile) which gives us 1 variable axis (column) and 1 column of data. Two columns are fixed. We could have following behaviour:
  - `convertmc dose.bdo` will produce plain ASCII file with 5 columns (X,Y,Z, data, errors)
  - `convertmc dose.bdo --plotdata` will produce file with 3 columns(Z,data, errors) automatically figuring out which column is variable. X and Y position can be saved to header.
for gnuplot it would work this way:
  - `convertmc dose.bdo --converter default, gnuplot` will file with 5 columns and gnuplot script reading from a such file.
  - `convertmc dose.bdo --converter default, gnuplot --plotdata` will produce file with 3 columns and gnuplot script reading from 3-columns file.

django.db.utils.ProgrammingError: permission denied to create extension "hstore"

This is easy to fix, of course, as a psql superuser do:

```
\connect your_database;
CREATE EXTENSION hstore;
```

and I think, it should be documented.

    fh = open('a.dex','rb')
    dex_data = fh.read()
    dx_dvm = dvm.DalvikVMFormat(dex_data)
    map_code = dx_dvm.map_list.get_item_type('TYPE_CODE_ITEM')
    print map_code.get_length()
except Exception as e:
    print(str(e))
```

- Androguard Version: 3.2
- Python Version: 2.7

issue_title: Email address and tool name missing from seqfetcher eutils call
issue_body:
On behalf of @andreasprlic:

The bioutils library makes request to the NCBI that are not valid according to their API spec.

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK25497/

NCBI requires email and tool parameters as part of URLs. These need to get registered with them for accessing eutils.

When sequences exist, add assembly name aliases.

Probably use bioutils for now to attach aliases like GRCh38:1 to ncbi:NC_000001.11. 
Might as well do patch levels too.

issue_title: Integrate the search subsystem with the API
issue_body:
After working on the search subsystem, I'm realizing that the developer UX can be improved significantly if we build a "fast sync" option for search as well. 

Similar to the [blockstack fast sync](https://github.com/blockstack/blockstack-core/issues/272) it can fetch an index (signed by developers) and immediately start serving search results in a new deployment. We can start a background process that creates a (trusted) index locally. 

Until the local index is created, yes, you're trusting the developers for the data you got but it's exactly the same security model as the general fast sync of Blockstack Core and can improve the UX a lot.

Keeping the discussion flag on this until I get more feedback and we get a yes/no decision for implementing this feature.

Not assigning a milestone right now.

issue_title: Explain why co-routine style is unsupported
issue_body:
This is really important if we want to use txaio that decorators to be supported.

Thanks
`txaio` is a pure Python package, and we should publish universal wheels (in addition to the source dist we publish today).

when visit the website http://autobahn.ws/python/, click the "Site", it shows the list twice...
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/4221783/11085893/6cccfabc-8886-11e5-94c6-766744133446.png)

There are multiple places within Autobahn (at the WebSocket and at the WAMP level) where we use timers sticked to protocol instances.

When running massive numbers of connections, this can lead to significant CPU load.

Since these timers don't need to be precise, and hence we can conserve CPU by batching timeouts into buckets (and process timeout callbacks within a bucket in chunks).

The underlying functionality will be in `txaio`: https://github.com/crossbario/txaio/issues/66

Once that is done, we should use them here.

I've had a stab at re-enabling the negative index notation for the RegistrySlices and have hit a problem:

I have a registry with 90 jobs with the jobIDs ranging from 1 to 353. i.e. lots of deleted jobs:

`jobs[-1:]` actually passes the slice `slice(90:sys.maxsize:None)`
(i.e. the `-1` string is pre-processed away at some level in IPython/ast as far as I can tell and `len(jobs)` returns 90.)

This causes a problem because doing the following works:
`jobs[90:]` gives `slice(90:sys.maxsize:None)`

This can be fixed by getting the `len(jobs)` to return `353` but this would cause problems due to then trying to do: `assert len(jobs[5:6]) == 2` as `len(jobs[5:6])` has to be 6 for `jobs[5:6][-1]` to return `jobs(6)`.

At this stage I'm thinking we can't sensibly enable negative indexes due to the problem of mapping these indices against the position in the registry dictionary.

Does anyone have any experience with this section of the code? I'm suspecting this could be an IPython feature(bug?) but I'm yet to test against IPython4.

issue_title: Add a file type which writes to a shared filesystem
issue_body:
It's common when running jobs on a local batch system to write the output files of the jobs manually to some shared file-system which is also accessible from the desktop machine of the user. We don't currently have a file type which supports this automatically. `LocalFile` will copy it over the sandbox so that is not what we want.

The closest we have is using a `MassStorageFile` with setting the `defaultProtocol` to `''`, the `uploadOptions.path` to the shared file-system and `uploadOptions.cp_cmd` etc. to `cp` etc.

Perhaps it would be worth making a new file type (e.g. `SharedFile`) which derives from `MassStorageFile` which sets these defaults to something sensible?

issue_title: GangaDirac should poll DIRAC for known SE
issue_body:
GangaDirac should poll the Dirac for known SE, else we will have to keep a configuration of the known SE in the DIRAC config.

For LHCb this is nice and short and _is just_ the Tier1 sites. For GridPP Dirac this is much larger and complex and it is more correct for GangaDirac to ask for known SE unless one is specified before doing something such as a put().

Hopefully this is nice and short and there is an API call to determine this, in which case I'll change the caching method in DiracFile to only ever request this list once as it's unlikely a site will go into downtime in any given session.
If this turns into something more difficult this may get pushed back but I would be surprised if this can't be achieved correctly in a few lines of code.

issue_body: let's suppose I have a **bucket named as local-task** and **hostname for localstack is awslocal.** I want to access **my bucket from windows browser** with the following address style **http://local-task.awslocal:4572** **instead of http://localaws:4572/local-task**  (with this format I am able to access the same)
Is there a way we can login using a token from previous login instead of using a password every time?

Since we're supporting both python2 and python3, our async options are very limited. I think we have to either use gevent, use threading, or maybe we can use something from the twisted stack?

Personally, I'd really like to just drop support for python2 (it is the way the ecosystem is moving anyway), and use either the 3.4 syntax of `@asyncio.coroutine` and `yield from` or 3.5+ of `async def` and `await`. It would give us much more semantic code. Maybe the right answer *is* just a community-maintained async fork for python 3.4+.

Issue created to separate out discussion from #126.

issue_title: Move buildozer to bin not scripts, update to more recent version
issue_body:
I'm going to wait for https://github.com/bazelbuild/buildtools/pull/208 to land before updating buildozer, because it will include some implicit name fixes.

Address the TODO in https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/cd228b7da2e572e7da19b492d8e248a5faf7d92e/contrib/buildrefactor/src/python/pants/contrib/buildrefactor/buildozer_binary.py#L22-L24

I would like to suggest the addition of an `additional_build_roots` option to pantsbuild. If this is already an option, please forgive my ignorance. 
## Motivation

As pantsbuild becomes more popular, we may expect that people who are writing applications in pantsbuild might want to leverage already existing pantsbuild applications. In the case that there is no other way to retrieve these applications, there should be an easy way to reference already existing pantsbuild setups within an existing project.
## The Problem

One might be able to see the dilemma. If I have my project, projectA, and an external library (which uses pantsbuild itself), libraryExt, I would attempt to integrate the two together putting their folders at the same level (Such that `projectA/` and `libraryExt/` are both in the build root).

Then, in order to depend on something that libraryExt provides, I insert something like `dependencies = [ "//libraryExt/src/python:feature" ]` into a `BUILD` file in projectA.

This is all well and good; however, when pants resolves `//libraryExt/src/python:feature`, it will then attempt to resolve dependencies of `//libraryExt/src/python:feature`, which are defined relative to libraryExt's build root. Pants will then exit and inform the user that it cannot find those targets.
## Current Solution

I have solved this problem by writing a generic script that rewrites all subproject's BUILD targets as relative to the parent project's build path.
## Proposed Solution

The addition of a `additional_build_roots` option, which takes a list of paths of other build roots. Upon not being able to resolve a target, pants will then attempt to resolve the target starting from roots listed in `additional_build_roots`.
## Possible Complications/Solutions
### Incorrect Target Resolution
#### Problem

This option could create some interesting behavior between same-named targets in different build roots. Consider the following scenario:

`projectA/src/python/BUILD` has `dependencies = [ "//src/python:nonexistent" ]`, which doesn't exist and should not exist.
`projectB/src/python/BUILD` contains the definition for the target "nonexistent" and `projectB/` is also enumerated as an `additional_build_root` in the options.

Thus, pants will successfully resolve the `nonexistent` target by finding it in projectB, even though this was not the intended behavior.
#### Solution

This problem presents itself because a target was resolved using a build root that it did not fall under. The purpose of this `additional_build_roots` is not to allow for cross-build-root behavior, but instead to resolve targets within the same build root.

Thus, pants should only attempt to resolve an unresolved target using an `additional_build_root` if the `BUILD` file that the target is called from also falls under the `additional_build_root`. 
# Conclusion

This feature would easily allow pants projects to be embedded within another. For single, monolithic projects, this does not have any unintended consequences. However, for projects with embedded subprojects (perhaps using Git Submodules, where one does not want to edit any files away from upstream), this allows clean integration.

As an edit, I would be happy to write this feature. However, I wanted to verify that it is actually worthy of implementation and that I've thought it through well-enough

issue_title: Improve cache hit rates in travis
issue_body:
master builds that hit travis's cache of the homedir still occasionally (always?) see:
* "A pants owned rustup installation could not be found, installing..."
* "Downloading" messages from cargo
* curl-y fetching messages (presumably for cmake/protoc?)

... but all of those should be included in the cached homedir.

----

After investigation, it seems that our caches have gotten too large, and so we're failing to upload them. Failing to upload them means we fail to download them later, which triggers re-compilation of many rust tools.

Downloading things is not necessarily a problem: the travis cache docs indicate that large, cheaply-installed things should be fetched each time rather than cached: so re-fetching cmake/protoc or even the rust toolchain each time is totally fine. But re-compiling the world is less fine.

issue_title: pantsd memory leak when used with v2 changed
issue_body: based on recent testing, it seems like the memory leak we saw before when fully priming a pre-fork `BuildGraph` is back when the daemon is used with v2 changed. it does not manifest with e.g. repeated `list ::`. I think this points to #4424 and the inherent non-daemon-safety of `BuildGraph` + `Target` as the culprit.

issue_title: Remove "average" boolean input to txn.get_turnover() function
issue_body:
Currently the get_turnover function accepts a boolean input 'Average=True/False' where -
 
Average==True triggers a code path that divides the day's traded value by 2 (thereby attempting to average the buys and sells placed by an algo in a given day). 

Average==False (default) leaves the traded value as is. 

For clarity we should remove this boolean so that we have only a single conventional definition for turnover = today's traded value ($) / today's total portfolio value ($)

As a follow up, verify that all current plots/computations in pyfolio display and use this default formulation so that we would not expect any of our past analyses to change. 
cc @twiecki

It looks like [enough has changed](https://github.com/quantopian/pyfolio/compare/v0.7.0...master) on pyfolio to merit v0.8.0 (with risk and performance attribution, I think we might even want to call it v1.0.0!)

We are also seeing an increasing number of users running into bugs that have already been patched: upping the version would make it easier to diagnose errors (both ours, and open source users).

To-do:
- [ ] Performance attribution tear sheet #417 (still WIP, will definitely want to wait for this)

issue_title: Model level validators don't work as expected
issue_body:
If you have a Model, with a validate function like this:

```python
class User(Model):
    def validate_email(self, data, value):
        return value.lower()
```

Returning the value does NOT affect the field on the object, or in `_data`.

So, for example, this would be False:

```python
u = User({'email': 'TEST@TEST.com'})
u.email = 'test@test.com'
```

This, however, does work:

```python
def validate_email(self, data, value):
    data['email'] = value.lower()
```

I've tried both 1.1.1 and the current pre-release.

There are a number of open issues regarding model validation. Unfortunately, it's an aspect of Schematics that's perhaps the most difficult to work on because the current system is somewhat confusing.

In addition to the issues already reported, there are a few details I'd like to ask about:

* Validating a model will reset any empty fields to their default values:

  ```python
  class M(Model):
      n = IntType(default=123)
  ```
  ```python
  >>> m = M()
  >>> m.n = None
  >>> m.to_primitive()
  {'n': None}
  >>> m.validate()
  >>> m.to_primitive()
  {'n': 123}
  ```

  Bug or not?

  (The first version of the Undefined feature introduced a new parameter called `apply_defaults`, which can be used to control this when importing and validating. However, the appropriate default setting for validation depends on whether the current behavior is by design or accident.)

* By the time `_check_for_unknown_fields()` is called from the main `validate()` function, `import_loop` will have already checked for rogue fields.

  What is then the purpose of `_check_for_unknown_fields()`?

* If a type-level validator fails, a model-level validator for the same field will not be called, as the field will have been dropped from `data` by then.

  Bug or not?

from schematics.serialize import (to_python, to_json)
ImportError: No module named serialize

I'm using simpleJSON in my project and I call some of the functions from 2 separate cpp files.
For some reason I get the linking issues: 

duplicate symbol __ZN4json5ArrayEv in: [...]
duplicate symbol __ZN4json6ObjectEv in: [...]
duplicate symbol __ZN4jsonlsERNSt3__113basic_ostreamIcNS0_11char_traitsIcEEEERKNS_4JSONE in: [...]
duplicate symbol __ZN4json4JSON4LoadERKNSt3__112basic_stringIcNS1_11char_traitsIcEENS1_9allocatorIcEEEE in: [...]

If I use it only in one cpp file it's OK.

issue_title: event files too large when TF graph contains constant data
issue_body:
TB 1.8.0
OS: any
Python version: any

Tensorboard event file abnormally large when the TF graph contains data defined with tf.constant.
For example for MNIST the event file will be almost 1GB if the MNIST dataset is loaded with tf.constant.  The mnist dataset is 50MB uncompressed.

Storing this data in the event files is never useful. In the Tensorboard interface, only small constants are visible. It is not planned to make large constant arrays visible as it would not be a useful feature. Large constant arrays should be dramatically truncated when stored in Tensorboard event files, or not stored at all.

### What did you do?

Execute these commands (Dockerfile) from a CentOS 7 base image:

```
RUN set -x \
  && pip install --upgrade pip \
  && pip install --upgrade setuptools \
  && pip install pexif==0.15 \
  && pip install -e git+https://github.com/thumbor/thumbor.git@master#egg=thumbor \
  && pip install opencv-engine==1.0.1 \
  && pip install thumbor-s3==0.2.0 \
  && pip install tc-prometheus==0.1.1 \
  && pip uninstall -y pillow || true \
  && pip install -U --force-reinstall pillow-simd \
  && python -c "from PIL import Image; print(Image.PILLOW_VERSION)" \
  && pip list
```

### What did you expect to happen?

That pillow-simd is a replacement package for pillow.

### What actually happened?

I get this this error message when running thumbor:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/bin/thumbor", line 6, in <module>

pkg_resources.DistributionNotFound: The 'Pillow<5.0.0,>=3.0.0' distribution was not found and is required by thumbor

issue_title: `shell` and related arguments to `run_command` and `exec_command` are problematic
issue_body:
I don't think the `shell` arguments to `run_command` and `exec_command` make sense -- you cannot know _a priori, in general_ which shell the remote host is running by default, which means (among other things) that you do not know what its escaping and quoting rules are.

As such, there's no guarantee that the `run_command` fragment below -- given a user-supplied shell and command (let alone attempting to use `$SHELL`) -- makes any sense on the remote system:
```
if shell:
    _command += '%s "%s"' % (shell, command,)
else:
    _command += '$SHELL -c "%s"' % (command,)
```

You could assume the remote is always entirely POSIX-shell-compatible, but in reality there's two common classes of system where that won't be true: users of `fish`, and many embedded devices. I don't think this is the right level at which to deal with the shell, because the requirements are very specific to the caller. 

Consider: currently, specifying any of the `shell`, `sudo`, or `user` arguments in either function alters the semantics of the `command` argument, because now the caller has to shell-escape the command string, and the escaping requirements may vary from shell to shell.

issue_title: Root logger at __init__.py causes all messages to be logged, regardless of the level
issue_body:
With calling logging.basicConfig() at __init__.py you create an root logger with NOTSET as level. This causes all messages to be logged, regardless of the level. This will be inherited to all child loggers (after importing nailgun)! 

I have debugged my code for hours without knowing why my logger logs debug messages while the level was set to INFO. 

Please don't use the root logger!

issue_title: How to run a job with 'qsub' arguments or script flags
issue_body:
When I run a script on an HPC with a scheduler such as SGE, my script might contain flags like these:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#$ -pe openmpi 32
#$ -A TensorFlow
#$ -N rqsub_tile
#$ -cwd
#$ -S /bin/bash
#$ -q gpu0.q
#$ -l excl=true

run_script.sh

Or, I might run a `qsub` command like this:

```bash
$ qsub -wd $PWD -o :${qsub_logdir}/ -e :${qsub_logdir}/ -j y -N "$job_name" -pe threaded 6-18 -l mem_free=10G -l mem_token=10G run_script.sh
```

issue_title: Empty lines can't trigger updates to the prompt
issue_body:
Currently entering an empty line completely bypasses all precmd and postcmd hooks, so there is no way to do something like update the prompt based on asynchronous notifications from a background thread when the user just presses enter.

postparsing_postcmd() should always be called, even for an empty line.

issue_title: Can't run Python scripts using "cmd" before entering an interactive Python session
issue_body:
There is currently a bug in the py command where the "cmd" function present in the embedded Python console isn't defined until after an interactive python session is entered once.

The fix is very easy, just move the relevant code a little bit earlier in the do_py() function.

issue_title: Can we do something better for macos testing?
issue_body:
Travis's MacOS support is dreadfully slow these days. I pushed a new commit to a PR at ~7:30pm California time, which is outside working hours everywhere in the US and Europe, and 2.5 hours later it still hasn't started running one of the MacOS jobs.

I don't think there's any way to bribe/bully/blackmail travis into working better, short of switching to one of [their private plans](https://travis-ci.com/plans). I guess we could ask. But they know about the problem, they have no capacity to add, and it's not like we particularly deserve special treatment somehow.

CircleCI offers a [free "seed" sized MacOS plan for OSS projects](https://circleci.com/pricing/#build-os-x). This gives 2x concurrency (which is all we need for any given job anyway), and 500 minutes a month of build time. On Travis currently we're at ~5 min of MacOS build time per commit; I don't know if CircleCI is comparable or not. But if so that would give us ~100 builds a month, which ... might be enough for our current needs? I'm a little nervous because our needs could change without much warning, and glancing over the travis build history right now my guesstimate is we're doing >50 builds a month, so there's not a ton of headroom. But it's probably worth trying, since the current setup is miserable.

Or we could try making puppy-dog eyes at @reaperhulk and see if he's willing to let us piggy-back on pyca/cryptography jenkins infrastructure.

issue_title: 'pip install' on our MacOS python 3.5 CI is flaky
issue_body:
Example:

https://ci.cryptography.io/blue/organizations/jenkins/python-trio%2Ftrio/detail/PR-503/12/pipeline/

```
Collecting pyOpenSSL (from -r test-requirements.txt (line 4))

  Could not find a version that satisfies the requirement pyOpenSSL (from -r test-requirements.txt (line 4)) (from versions: )
```

Example:

https://ci.cryptography.io/blue/organizations/jenkins/python-trio%2Ftrio/detail/PR-503/8/pipeline/

```
Collecting pytest>=3.3 (from -r test-requirements.txt (line 1))

  Could not find a version that satisfies the requirement pytest>=3.3 (from -r test-requirements.txt (line 1)) (from versions: )
```

This is the same kind of message that you get when using an old pip on an old MacOS, and it can't handle PyPI's new TLS requirements. And it's suspicious that it seems to happen on the Python 3.5 builder only (so far). However... in both of those runs you can see that the first thing we do is use `get-pip.py` to upgrade to pip 10.0.1. The log verifies it:

```
++ pip --version
pip 10.0.1 from /Users/jenkins/workspace/-503-TALZSOV5BV3A6O3/trio/.venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pip (python 3.5)
```

Which makes sense, because if we didn't have working TLS, then we would be 100% unsuccessful at downloading things. Here we're only... intermittently, apparently at random, unsuccessful at downloading things.

I guess the first thing to do is to add some `-vvv` switches to the pip calls and wait for it to happen again...

Kind of annoying, e.g. this is a doc-only PR: https://codecov.io/gh/python-trio/trio/pull/139/changes

The solution is probably to write proper tests for acontextmanager.

issue_title: Calling ray.register_class after defining a remote function fails and (sometimes) gives no error message.
issue_body:
An example like the following sometimes works and sometimes fails (I haven't been able to get this particular example to fail, but a similar one did on a different machine).

```python
import ray
ray.init()

class Foo(object):
  def __init__(self):
    pass

@ray.remote
def f():
  return Foo()

@ray.remote
def g(x):
  return 1

ray.register_class(Foo)

ray.get([f.remote() for _ in range(10)] + [g.remote(Foo()) for _ in range(10)])
```

If the worker tries to execute the function `f` or `g` before the `register_class` call has operated on the worker, then it will throw an exception.

Normally, it should fail with the following error message.

```python
Remote function __main__.f failed with:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/rkn/Workspace/ray/python/ray/worker.py", line 1634, in process_task
    store_outputs_in_objstore(return_object_ids, outputs, worker)
  File "/Users/rkn/Workspace/ray/python/ray/worker.py", line 1973, in store_outputs_in_objstore
    worker.put_object(objectids[i], outputs[i])
  File "/Users/rkn/Workspace/ray/python/ray/worker.py", line 474, in put_object
    numbuf.store_list(objectid.id(), self.plasma_client.conn, [value])
  File "/Users/rkn/Workspace/ray/python/ray/serialization.py", line 97, in serialize
    raise Exception("Ray does not know how to serialize objects of type {}. To fix this, call 'ray.register_class' with this class.".format(type(obj)))
Exception: Ray does not know how to serialize objects of type <class '__main__.Foo'>. To fix this, call 'ray.register_class' with this class.
```

However, I have seen it fail with just `None` (that's what showed up in `ray.error_info()`.

Inspecting the event_log a little more closely (for a different script that gave rise to this problem) gave the error 

```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/ubuntu/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ray-0.0.1-py2.7.egg/ray/worker.py", line 1542, in process_task
    arguments = get_arguments_for_execution(worker.functions[function_id.id()], args, worker)
  File "/home/ubuntu/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ray-0.0.1-py2.7.egg/ray/worker.py", line 1852, in get_arguments_for_execution
    argument = worker.get_object([arg])[0]
  File "/home/ubuntu/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ray-0.0.1-py2.7.egg/ray/worker.py", line 455, in get_object
    0)
  File "/home/ubuntu/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ray-0.0.1-py2.7.egg/ray/serialization.py", line 127, in deserialize
    cls = whitelisted_classes[class_id]
KeyError: 'autocore.yaml_config.YamlConfig'
```

The failure in this case appears to be when an argument is deserialized. We should do several things.

1. Make sure we propagate error messages from this case.
2. Make sure we provide better utilities for inspecting tasks because there might be errors in the event log that are not propagated.
3. Add tests for this problem.

issue_title: First class API support for evm
issue_body:
Ethereum support is somewhat hacked onto the side of "regular" manticore right now. Ethereum has first class support in the command line, but not in the API.

To get a feel for this, just look at the imports:

Linux usage: `from manticore import Manticore`
Ethereum usage: `from manticore.ethereum import ManticoreEVM`

a few things just feel wrong about this...

- what is the relationship between `Manticore` and `ManticoreEVM`? 
- Why is there no `ManticoreLinux`?
- Why is there no `from manticore.linux import ...`
- why does Linux support reside at the API top level, but ethereum in a submodule?

it is fairly clear that there is an inequality in terms of the Linux and eth APIs, which doesn't make sense as they are simply two different execution platforms that are logically at the same hierarchy level. there should be some amount of symmetry in the APIs for using each.

this will probably require a fairly radical restructure, that would warrant a minor version bump, to 0.2.0.

### historical notes

how did the linux api get like this? the `Manticore` class was the easiest path forward to make manticore more programmable, and not just a command line tool. it was our first attempt at creating a manticore API, and at this point we should reconsider how much sense this design makes, especially given new requirements for eth support.

how did the eth api get like this? historically, when eth support first started, the eth api was first created in examples/evm/seth.py. that file included `ManticoreEVM`, a `Manticore` subclass implementing a sort of Eth api object. that file was simply moved into the manticore package as manticore/ethereum.py. now that we've successfully gotten ethereum support working at all, we should take some time to think about how to properly fit ethereum support into the codebase such that it has first class api support, and that is congruent with Linux.

Application log: https://gist.github.com/54879fc9932ac1579265f23b27d28227

Hi,

this might be the wrong place for this discussion. In case please suggest a more appropriate one.

We have some special requirements which we plan to build on top of pyfs2, and we hope you have some good ideas on how to achieve / implement this.
- We only need to read the data
- A scandir like function that **transparently** treats container files (*.tar.gz; *.zip) very much like a directory
- Whatever the iterator returns must include the full "virtual" path, e.g. /xxx/data.tar.gz/subdir/myfile and the fd related to the file (please see below for why that is)
- Some of our tar.gz files are (very) large and we'd like to use streaming (mode="r|*"). Based on previous experience this is significantly faster for our use case and setup. Streaming however comes with a caveat: seek()/tell() is not allowed and thus the iteration order is the order in which the files were added to the container file. That limitation is fine for us. 
- Due to the streaming requirement the my_scandir iterator needs to return a tuple of fd and info, rather then the filename. Using the filename to open requires seek/tell.
- This fd + info approach raises some issues with nested container files, because pyfs2 fs.open(path) does not support fd/fileobj, but only path. Python Tarfile does support tarfile.open(name=.., fileobj=...). This is required for nested container files only (unfortunately we are not able to change that).
- To my understanding streaming is currently only supported in python for tar-files , not for zip files. Which is fine for us. Where not supported, silently apply fd = fs.open(path).
- It is fine for us if the fd is a binary stream. We apply TextIOWrapper at a later stage where necessary

Do you have some good ideas on how to easily achieve this?

thanks a lot
Juergen

issue_title: support a few more operations on files, to help with losing `build`
issue_body:
Now that `build` is going/gone, the rules fields that remain are `export` and `files`. We've been talking about adding a few new fields to handle the most generic things people have been doing with `build`. I propose this set, in order of operation:

- `move`: a map of old paths to new paths
- `keep`: a replacement for `files` with a better name, though note it comes before `export`
- `executable`: a list of files to run `chmod +x` on (note that git trees only allow 644 or 755)
- `export`: same as before
- `files`: same as before, *deprecated*

It's pretty clear we want `move` to happen before anything else, especially before `export`, so that it runs while you still have all the files you started with. With that in mind, I think it makes the most sense to have *everything* happen before `export`, in the name of consistency. It would be confusing to have some fields take pre-export full paths, and others take a post-export shortened paths. The shortened paths were a bit magical anyway. This means that `keep` won't be just a renaming of `files`; it'll also take full paths instead of short paths.

Syntax-wise, `move` seems like it can only be a map of paths to paths. `keep` and `executable` can probably keep the single-glob-or-list-of-globs syntax that we currently have with `files`. It probably makes sense for executable to ignore directories, or maybe they should be an error?

@olson-sean-k also suggested a `copy` field, similar to `move` but preserving the source paths. If he wants to implement that, I have no objections :)

issue_title: manual commits only after successful message production
issue_body:
Description
===========

I expect a relatively common use case for Kafka is to consume a message, process it, and publish the results to a different topic (let’s say, one published message for every one message consumed). I am wondering if I am overlooking “the easy way” to use manual commits to do this while guaranteeing at least once processing/publishing of every message consumed.

The default auto-commit strategy doesn’t seem to satisfy this guarantee.The solution also seems more nuanced than manually committing the relevant consumer message offset in the producer’s delivery report callback. Both of those strategies seem like they would violate the at least once processing/publishing guarantee if I:
- asynchronously publish message `a` associated with consumer offset x
- asynchronously publish message `b` associated with consumer offset x + 1
- commit offset x + 1 (at some interval, or on successful delivery report for `b`) which also effectively commits offset x
- get a failed delivery report for `a`

Given the assumptions above, the logic required to meet this guarantee seems tricky for what I expect is a typical use case. Currently, I have logic in the delivery report callback that tracks pending commits for each TopicPartition and only finalizes a commit when there are no smaller offsets still outstanding.

Am I overlooking something? Maybe librdkafka has the necessary intelligence in its implementation of `commit()` without my help (though I could not find evidence of it after a quick search), or maybe this is handled by Kafka’s offset tracking?

```
>>> confluent_kafka.version()
('0.11.0', 720896)
>>> confluent_kafka.libversion()
('0.11.0', 721151)
```
Broker version: 0.11.0
Consumer configuration:
```

    ...
    'enable.auto.commit': False
}
```

issue_body:
Both TAR and ZIP archive files can require a codepage for file entry names

* [ ] add means to set file system encoding

I want to override LEAFLET_CONFIG for specific custom widget, which would be used in specific LeafletGeoAdmin.

Is it possible to do that without reimplementing ModelForm? Here how it's looks currently:
my `widgets.py`:
```python
from leaflet.forms.widgets import LeafletWidget

class ClientLocationWidget(LeafletWidget):    
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):      
        attrs_override = {
                          'settings_overrides':

                           'DEFAULT_ZOOM': 12
                           }
                        }
        super().__init__(attrs = attrs_override) 
        #what should I do with original *args, **kwargs?
```
my `admin.py`:
```python
class ClientAdmin(LeafletGeoAdmin):
    widget = ClientLocationWidget
    #all other mundane stuff...
```

But it has no effect.

## Use Case

When experimenting with data loading from a Data Cube users need to be able to
save the `xarray.Dataset` objects back into an Index, for use in future analysis.

- Interactive use with small amounts of data which fit in RAM.
- Requires write access to a Data Cube Index.
- The `Product` may or may not already exist.
- No support for including lineage/sources for derived datasets.

## Starting Point

@petewa has an initial implementation available [in the `csiro/execution-engine` branch](https://github.com/opendatacube/datacube-core/blob/csiro/execution-engine/datacube/api/datacube_save.py#L33)
with an API that looks like:

```

    ds = DatacubeSave(dc)
    ds.save(nbar, 'my_bucket', 's3aio', 'dcsave_mydata', 'eo',
            chunking = {'time': 1, 'x': 3, 'y': 3})
    ds.save(nbar, '/home/ubuntu/data/output', 's3aio_test', 'dcsave_mydata', 'eo',
            chunking = {'time': 1, 'x': 3, 'y': 3})
    ds.save(nbar, '/home/ubuntu/data/output', 'NetCDF CF', 'dcsave_mydata', 'eo',
            chunking = {'time': 1, 'x': 4, 'y': 4})

```

This is a good starting point for implementing a simple `save` function.

In discussion with @Kirill888 there's a few additions and changes we would propose:
- Include the `save()` function directly on the `Datacube` object.
- Split the API so that `create_product` is a separate step to `save`
  - Simplifies the function definition, and can raise errors earlier.
- Add more rigorous error checking
  - eg. does the dataset match the product?
- More test cases

## Potential Problems

- There can be problems with the auto-matching of `Products`, which would become
  far more likely with users creating many more products. Will raise another
  issue about this, but it should be fixed before releasing a `save` feature.

issue_title: [OSX] skbuild.setup defaults cmake_args without checking kwargs for the arguments.
issue_body:
OS: OSX/Darwin (testing on travis).

`setup` checks the command line arguments for `-DCMAKE_OSX_DEPLOYMENT_TARGET` and for `-DCMAKE_OSX_ARCHITECTURES`, and if they don't exist, it defaults them [here](https://github.com/scikit-build/scikit-build/blob/d8e4db57e0a930db75bd204e47b64ba83a7300bb/skbuild/setuptools_wrap.py#L461-L477). The issue with this is that if the user passed either of those arguments to setup via a kwarg. i.e. `setup(..., cmake_args=[..., "-DCMAKE_OSX_DEPLOYMENT_TARGET:STRING=10.7"])`, it gets overwritten with the default, even when nothing had been passed to the command line. 
This causes builds to break for no apparent reason (see the reasons for [this PR](https://github.com/skvark/opencv-python/pull/110)). A simple fix is moving the lines where the command line arguments are added to the arguments passed via kwarg from [here](https://github.com/scikit-build/scikit-build/blob/d8e4db57e0a930db75bd204e47b64ba83a7300bb/skbuild/setuptools_wrap.py#L503-L506) to right above the block mentioned above. 
Since `cmake_args` is not referenced between line 477 and line 503, nothing will get more args than it expected, and CLI will still override the args passed via kwarg, but default will only be set if neither the CLI or kwarg `cmake_arg` are set.
If this is approved I will [PR the fix](https://github.com/yonip/scikit-build/commit/2546c18c246857679e469b2a84576e1f802baa65) described above. 
(This is an issue first because the contribution guidelines specify filing bugs before trying to fix them).

issue_title: threshold_li fails for "zeros" input images due to undefined `threshold`
issue_body:
## Description

When running `skimage.filters.threshold_li`, if the input image is all 0, the 
`tolerance` is set to zero causing the while loop never to execute and a somewhat cryptic error is
encountered because `threshold` never gets set. 

A typical traceback is 

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 3, in <module>
    threshold_li(np.zeros((100,100)))
  File "./skimage/filters/thresholding.py", line 509, in threshold_li
    return threshold + immin
UnboundLocalError: local variable 'threshold' referenced before assignment
```

I recognise that this is a special case, but I just wanted to raise this issue as a way of
asking 

> do we want this somewhat cryptic error, or should we catch this 
> using a check on the input image"?  
 

## Way to reproduce
All that's needed to reproduce this (Python 3.4.1, skimage 0.13dev) is: 

```
import numpy as np
from skimage.filters import threshold_li
threshold_li(np.zeros((100,100)))
```

## Description
Currently we have a few travis parameters that pertain to handling how the tests/documentation behaves for different configurations.

These include:
  - `MINIMUM_REQUIREMENTS`
  - `OPTIONAL_DEPS`
  - `BUILD_DOCS`

I think the only necessary one is `MINIMUM_REQUIREMENTS`. The others were necessary for 2.7/3 compatibility. 

I think we should cleanup the logic such that 
  - `OPTIONAL_DEPS = not MINIMUM_REQUIREMENTS`
  - `BUILD_DOCS = not MINIMUM_REQUIREMENTS`

issue_title: [Enhancement request] PasswordField (or other mechanism to change a password value)
issue_body:
**User story:** I am a Django site developer. I want to model LDAP password fields, so that users can modify their LDAP passwords using my Django application.

**Suggestion:** Create a new PasswordField field type. It probably only needs write access, and any modifications are passed through to the LDAP server for handling.

**Existing work:**

There is an existing Pull Request ( https://github.com/django-ldapdb/django-ldapdb/pull/46 ) that does this. It looks like it takes responsibility for hashing the password, then stores the result in LDAP.

An alternative would be to let the LDAP server handle the hashing. It looks like pyldap_orm does this with their `PasswordModify` class, defined in https://github.com/asyd/pyldap_orm/blob/master/pyldap_orm/controls.py and used in `LDAPModelUser` in https://github.com/asyd/pyldap_orm/blob/master/pyldap_orm/models.py

issue_title: Allow permission restrictions on staging/<jobid> directory?
issue_body:
I'm using pulsar to get around the limitation of Galaxy DRMAA as a real user, where the Galaxy datasets need to be world-readable. This is not acceptable for our user base. I have the following solution in use.... 

* Galaxy files dir is 660 galaxy:galaxy - so that galaxy itself can see everything, but no user on the cluster can access anyone else's dataset.

* Pulsar is being used to submit external real-user DRMAA jobs, where the 'copy' file action is used so that the private galaxy files/* are staged and available to the job. Anything not private, such as genome indices is using the 'none' action as is world-readable.

* Galaxy/pulsar can always access what they need, as relevant files have group ownership 'galaxy'. 

* The pulsar chown functionality gives neccessary real-user rights when jobs run under SLURM on the cluster.

* Am running galaxy and pulsar under umask 0007 so that dataset files aren't created world-readable. This ensures they aren't world readable when copied into the pulsar job staging dir.

This seems to be working nicely. However, instead of relying on umask it would be nice if it was possible to restrict the permissions on the `staging/<jobid>` directories, which always seem to be completely open (drwxrwxrwx).

I looked around, but couldn't find any configuration option, or obvious place where the perms are set for these job staging dirs.

Would it be possible to allow these dirs to be created (drwxrwx---) so that access can be restricted without relying on the umask when the staged datasets were originally created by galaxy?

Would be happy to work on this for a PR if I can get a pointer r.e. where the open perms on the staging dirs are set - I've looked but I'm lost.
Review discarding jobs at startup and make sure Pulsar sends messages back to Galaxy indicating that jobs are being discarded if it cannot verify that postprocessing has completed.

issue_title: Galaxy job failure: bogus escape: u'\\39'
issue_body:
The galaxy job submission to pulsar is failing where the job directory on the pulsar side begins with problematic two-digit numbers (like 39) on Windows. My pulsar job id numbers were 39? and all these attempts to submit from galaxy to pulsar failed. Once the job numbers got to 40? the failures stopped.

I think the issue is that the regular expression library is interpreting windows paths with embedded real backslashes incorrectly 'blah\\\\391\\\\working' as a special escape sequence (that is not valid). I'm not sure why \\\\38 and \\\\40 are more reasonable, but it suggests there is some regular expression escaping that is needed here (or different job directory names).

issue_title: Recursive shed uploads die if yaml is invalid rather than emitting error
Namespace packages need shared namespace declaration
issue_body:
We need this for the __init__.py in google so it can co-exist with other google.* libraries (from http://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/setuptools.html)

    __import__('pkg_resources').declare_namespace(__name__)

Secondly google.datalab should not be marked as a namespace package in setup.py, because we have top-level classes (eg. Context) in google.datalab.

A few CSV sources have a bunch of bogus rows that don't contain valid coordinates. Right now those spam the logs, one debug entry per bad input row. It'd be nice to suppress those log messages at least in obvious cases where the inputs aren't a number.

````
es-32628/output.txt:2015-01-18 23:34:31,852    DEBUG: Could not reproject lon lat in SRS EPSG:32628
us-or-portland/output.txt:2015-01-18 23:31:37,637    DEBUG: Could not reproject None None in SRS EPSG:2913
````

sources that do this: es-25830 es-25831 es-32628 us-or-portland

issue_title: worker process occasionally stops working, task is lost
issue_body:
With the new multiprocessing `jobs.py` there's an occasional bug that we can't reproduce.

The main visible effect is the run will idle at the end, reporting almost all the work is done. However no work is being processed and that whole run is stuck.
````
I 2015-01-18 23:59:39,533   MainThread     jobs:126  Job completion: 577/578 = 99%
I 2015-01-18 23:59:54,533   MainThread     jobs:126  Job completion: 577/578 = 99%
````
If that happens you can still salvage the work by doing a `kill -USR1` on the parent process. That will abort all work and start generating a result report and upload data to S3.

I think there's a related thing which I've observed once or twice with `htop`, which is that there's a worker process sitting around that hasn't changed its name nor has it been assigned work. You can kill this idle worker just fine with a SIGTERM and the pool will start a new one which will do useful work again. But it may be that whatever task was supposed to be assigned to that worker has now gotten lost.

I don't know how to reproduce this bug; it's occurred twice in about 20 runs I've done. It could be a bug in `mulitprocessing.Pool` or it could be that somehow we screwed things up by not executing a job cleanly. If we could reproduce it, the thing to do is attach a debugger and start looking at the internal state of the pool and see if there's something obviously wrong.

issue_title: SR300 multible camera couses freezes in uvc-wmf.h
issue_body:
| Required Info  |   |
|---|---|
| Camera Model | SR300 | 
| Firmware Version |  3.15.0.0 | 
| Operating System & Version |  Windows 10   |
| Build System |  VS2014 | 

Currently I'm experimenting with 5 RS300 Cameras, each of them connected to it's own usb controler.
It's working fine for most situations, but does couse a program freeze at some points ( sometimes, but the more cameras are connected, the more often they occure )

Setting parameters via

`void set_control(device & device, const extension_unit & xu, uint8_t ctrl, void *data, int len)` (uvc-wmf.h)

can freeze the whole program without a chance of recovering.

This can be reproduced by attaching more than one camera and simple keep activating and deactivating the laser of one of the camera.

Once one of the RS300 did crash the context is no longer able to be created ( even after restarting the program)
It will freeze at 

`WinUsb_WritePipe(usb_interface_handle, endpoint, (PUCHAR)buffer, bufferLength, &lengthWritten, NULL); ` (uvc-wmf.cpp)

sometimes during the **usb_synchronous_write** but also at **usb_synchronous_read** .

These problems do not occure using the F200 cameras ( tried it with 7 F200 cameras without any problems)

I made these changes to the ```librealsense/examples/cpp-capture.cpp``` and still I am facing similar issue. I connected the camera to USB 3.0 port.  

I am attaching the screenshot of the program (please see the top left corner of each stream for the FPS)

I thought I'd share an experience I just had with the R200 so that others who run into this problem wouldn't have to figure it out themselves.

I had a problem with an R200 sensor on one Windows 8.1 computer where my own application and all of the provided samples failed with the message "no matching media type" (this comes from the set_subdevice_mode function in uvc-wmf.cpp). On other Win 8.1 computers (also Win 10) the same sensor with the same DCM version works perfectly. (I tried nearly all of the available DCM versions as well while trying to figure this problem out without any difference to the result.)

After many hours of searching the only difference between the computers I found was that in device manager the Microsoft UVC driver for the R200 devices (Depth, Left-Right, RGB) had a different version. The computer with the problem had the version 6.3.9600.16384 (21.6.2006), while the computers that worked had the version 6.3.9600.17217 (same date 21.6.2006 though). I normally have automatic Windows updates turned off on the problematic computer, so as a last resort I thought to apply all important and recommended available windows updates. To my surprise after the updates the sensor started working perfectly and the UVC driver version had also changed to match the other computers.

**Summary:** All samples fail on Win 8.1 with the message "no matching media type". Reason is most probably the UVC driver version 6.3.9600.16384, which doesn't play nicely with the R200 sensor. Windows update fixes the issue and changes UVC driver version to 6.3.9600.17217.

| Required Info  |   |
|---|---|
| Camera Model | R200 | 
| Firmware Version |  1.0.72... | 
| Operating System & Version | Windows 8.1 Pro  |
| Build System |  VS2013 |

issue_title: attr_set should allow boolean and numeric pass-through
issue_body:
Attributes set via `attr_set` all end up being strings. However, when numerics and booleans are passed unquoted to the API, they will be set to their respective type. We should find a way to respect this, either by splitting `attr_set` into something along the lines of `attr_bool_set`, `attr_str_set`, and `attr_num_set`, or doing inference.

Even though I think the latter option is "prettier", the former seems the safest, especially if we alias `attr_set` to `attr_str_set` to maintain backwards compatibility, as it seems like configs will coerce type as able when input parameters are set to attributes.

issue_title: Document that the CLI's api is not consistent with regards to pip/wheel args and that this might change in the future.
issue_body: Wagon's CLI's structure is not consistent with pip/wheel. For instance, providing a requirements file for wagon requires using the `-a '-r requirements.txt'` flag. Currently, this is not so important as the main idea by the `-a` flag is to allow users to pass any arguments to pip or wheel. At some point, we should make this API consistent.

I think there's a problem with removing cookies: if we get them via ``splash:get_cookies``, then if the cookie is set to an empty string (as a result of logout), it will be absent in ``response.data['cookies']``, and as a result, they will stay in the ``cookiejar`` in ``SplashCookiesMiddleware``: https://github.com/lopuhin/scrapy-splash/commit/478db5d729adb027c43d72915c98aacb75bbbc64

I see three ways to fix this:

1. if the cookie disappeared from ``response.data['cookies']``, remove it from the ``cookiejar`` too
2. if the cookie disappeared from ``response.data['cookies']``, add it to ``cookiejar`` with an empty string value?
3. get cookies in some other way, for example from ``har`` log?

Not sure which way is the best. (1) is by far the easiest and looks very logical, but this might be different from what people will expect?

issue_body:
Hello, beginner issue I'm afraid. This is the 3rd WYSIWIG editor I have installed and is the best so far in terms of ease of installation and use. However, When I go to use it on my blog app it completely changes the styling of the templates where the output is being displayed (I did see this behaviour with the two other WYSIWYG editors, amongst other strange issues that lead me to uninstall them). 

I'm now into my second day of trying to find out what the problem is and fix it, but I'm not getting very far, although, adding |safe to the template is what appears to trigger the change (I know I need |safe here as leaving the HTML is not an option). I have attached an image of what the page looks like before and after adding |safe:

After |safe:

I am using Django 2.0.2 & Python 3.4.6. My styling uses Bootstrap 4, the list view of the blog is using cards in a 3 column x 4 row format. I am using a forloop to generate this.

I realise this is not necessarily specific to CKeditor given that I've seen this with other editors, but if you have any ideas that would really be appreciated as currently I'm getting fed up of banging my head against a brick wall. It's either a compatibility issue, or I'm missing something very simple.

At present, the template structure seems very un-Django-like. There are two base templates base.html and public_base.html, both of which {% include navigation.html %}. The Django template language doesn't allow one to define blocks in an include file. (see Stack Overflow 3983872) Pasting a copy of navigation.html into both base files is not attractive either.

I've rough-and-ready verified that it is possible to refactor this as a single base file basebase.html (much like public_base.html, with added, mostly empty, blocks), and a navigation.html that extends basebase.html, and then new versions of public_base.html and base.html which extend navigation.html and inject differences by defining or extending basebase- and navigation-defined blocks. My intent is to make it easier for the end users to customize appearance of the helpdesk and local content of the navigation bar, without having to make changes to the underlying base files that will be awkward to propagate across upgrades.

These changes will of course be awkward to propagate if there are already other plans afoot for these templates in future releases. Are there?  Is there any interest in this for the project?

issue_title: Support other activation in recurrent layers.
issue_body:
Currently in recurrent layers including SimpleRNN, LSTM, GRU and ConvLSTM, we hard code the activation to be `Tanh`, and inner_activation to be `Sigmoid`.
In Keras, users can pass in activation and inner_activation as parameters. Moreover, inner_activation is set to `hard_sigmoid` by default. Usually it turns out that when users add a recurrent layer, they won't specify the activation, namely they will use `hard_sigmoid` as inner_activation and we don't support it.
Maybe we can consider adding activation parameters in these layers.

issue_title: Unifier class: `unify` method returns duplicated patches
issue_body:
Unifier class: `unify` method returns a list containing duplicated patches in the case of conflicts containing equal patches (same path).

e.g.
```
patch1 = ('remove', '', [('a', 'b')])
patch2 = ('remove', '', [('a', 'b')])
conflicts = Conflict(patch1, patch2)
conflicts.take = 'f' # can be 's' too
```

The result of calling `u.unify([patch1], [patch2], [conflicts])` will be `[patch1, patch2]` instead of `[patch1]`
. This can lead to `KeyError`s in case of calling `patch`, as it will try to remove the same key twice. The second time it fails as the key has already been deleted.

issue_body:
A combination of the SQLAlchemy-Continnuum versioning support and initialising multiple Flask applications seems to the the source of these warnings:

...sqlalchemy/ext/declarative/clsregistry.py:120: SAWarning: This declarative base already contains a class with the same class name and module name as flask_sqlalchemy.PageVersion, and will be replaced in the string-lookup table.
  item.__name__
...sqlalchemy/orm/properties.py:194: SAWarning: On mapper Mapper|PageVersion|pages_page_version, primary key column 'pages_page_version.transaction_id' is being combined with distinct primary key column 'pages_page_version.transaction_id' in attribute 'transaction_id'.  Use explicit properties to give each column its own mapped attribute name.
  self.columns[0], self.key))
...sqlalchemy/orm/properties.py:194: SAWarning: On mapper Mapper|PageListVersion|pages_pagelist_version, primary key column 'pages_pagelist_version.transaction_id' is being combined with distinct primary key column 'pages_pagelist_version.transaction_id' in attribute 'transaction_id'.  Use explicit properties to give each column its own mapped attribute name.
  self.columns[0], self.key))
...sqlalchemy/orm/properties.py:194: SAWarning: On mapper Mapper|RecordMetadataVersion|records_metadata_version, primary key column 'records_metadata_version.transaction_id' is being combined with distinct primary key column 'records_metadata_version.transaction_id' in attribute 'transaction_id'.  Use explicit properties to give each column its own mapped attribute name.
  self.columns[0], self.key))
```

- The warnings are issued when Invenio-DB is [initialized here](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-db/blob/master/invenio_db/ext.py#L98).

What happens is:
1. Flask UI app is created and Invenio-DB is initialised first time (above mentioned initialisation step is not reached).
2. Flask REST app is created (and Invenio-DB is initialised a second time (now above initialisation step is reached).

The reason for above initialisation step was because sometimes loading order would prevent the versioning tables from being created (see 336a7ee7143fca86a15adddeaa17676788ea1099 - this was quite a pain to debug).

cc @jacquerie

issue_title: global: support mutable URLs in metadata
issue_body:
This is a question I have been asking myself for some time and I wonder if we could find a common solution to this problem.

This is not a feature needed immediately. This issue's goal is to start the discussion and see if there is any easy solution. It might be even that this is not an issue at all.

**Problem Description:**
- We currently store some URLs in the record. Example: the '$schema'.
- We want to give access to every past version of a record.
- Many services want to archive records "for ever".

**Question:**
Do we want the URLs to be valid for every version of a record? If yes, do we just want to change what the REST API returns or do we also want to be able to have up to date URLs internally?

**Concrete use cases:**
- The organization running the service changes its name and its URL is changed too.
- An organization shuts down and the records are moved to another one for preservation. (Example: from one B2Share instance to another)

Do we want to still give access to the old record schemas referenced by `$schema`, and if yes how do we do it?

The situation can be quite messy if we consider that records can be reverted to old versions and have possibly invalid `$schema` URLs.

Of course there are some URLs which we can't control as they are external.

**Note:**
This can also be true for URLs inside the record's schema, as they can reference other schemas and we can store a schema inside the record itself.

**Possible solution 1:**
There could be a module which provide a *mapping* from every old url to new ones.

Pros:
- it works for external URLs too.
- it does not modify the history of a record directly. It could be called by the REST API for each request, with some caching. The replacement could be optional in order to still have access to the old version.

**Possible solution 2:**
We could support variables in the URLs, like a *HOSTNAME* variable. They would be replaced for every request.

Pros:
- simple to implement. The variables can even be replaced by the serializers. We just need to call the substitution function before retrieving fields like `$schema`.

Cons:
- this is more complicated for complex dynamic URLs and external URLs as we can't list them all as variables.

**Possible solution 3:**
If we don't care about the past version's URL internally we can just preprocess the records before sending them to the user. Those preprocessors could be called in the default invenio-records-rest serializer.

@jirikuncar @lnielsen @tiborsimko What do you think?

issue_title: Feature Request: Whitelist lines ending in # nosec
issue_body:
So both [detect-secrets](https://github.com/Yelp/detect-secrets/blob/master/detect_secrets/plugins/high_entropy_strings.py#L41) and Bandit have the concept of whitelisting a line by putting a comment at the end, similar to how you've probably seen people do `# noqa: F401` or whatever, with pylint.

Let us steal once again, from Bandit, since they are most similar to us, [here are the relevant lines](https://github.com/openstack/bandit/blob/8f09d8b208f037b7d49ed6bc88f2ac200e7cc06c/bandit/core/manager.py#L270-L277), but we shall change `lineno + 1 for` to `enumerate(lines, start=1)` because it is more pythonic.

They also have the `--ignore-nosec        do not skip lines with # nosec comments` command line option` so we shall pass in the set of lines to the 2 calls to `find_vulnerabilities` [in \_\_main\_\_](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/blob/master/pyt/__main__.py#L53),

issue_title: Deleting a single letter within k-input leaves cursor at end of last word
issue_body:
String is being trimmed too aggressively, likely due to our use of `v-model.trim`. 

<!--
Instructions:
 * Fill out the sections below, replace …'s with information about your issue
 * Use the 'preview' function above this text box to verify formatting before submitting
-->

### Observed behavior
<!--
Description of the behavior that was observed, including screenshots or other references when applicable
-->

In any `k-input`, deleting a single bookending letter automatically throws the cursor to the next word. 

### Expected behavior
<!--
Description of what behavior was expected but did not occur
-->

Deleting a single letter only deletes that letter, Not the space before or after it.

### User-facing consequences
<!--
Implications and real-world consequences for learners, coaches, admins, and other users of the application
-->

Unexpected naming/renaming. Frustration.


### Steps to reproduce
<!--
